solution:
The minimum value of $T$ is 29.

Write $t_{n+1} = t_0+T$ and define $s_k = t_k-t_{k-1}$ for $1\leq k\leq n+1$. On $[t_{k-1},t_k]$, we have $f'(t) = k(t-t_{k-1})$ and so $f(t_k)-f(t_{k-1}) = \frac{k}{2} s_k^2$. Thus if we define
\[
g(s_1,\ldots,s_{n+1}) = \sum_{k=1}^{n+1} ks_k^2,
\]
then we want to minimize $\sum_{k=1}^{n+1} s_k = T$ (for all possible values of $n$) subject to the constraints that $g(s_1,\ldots,s_{n+1}) = 4045$ and $s_k \geq 1$ for $k \leq n$.

We first note that a minimum value for $T$ is indeed achieved. To see this, note that the constraints $g(s_1,\ldots,s_{n+1}) = 4045$ and $s_k \geq 1$ place an upper bound on $n$. For fixed $n$, the constraint $g(s_1,\ldots,s_{n+1}) = 4045$ places an upper bound on each $s_k$, whence the set of $(s_1,\ldots,s_{n+1})$ on which we want to minimize $\sum s_k$ is a compact subset of $\mathbb{R}^{n+1}$.

Now say that $T_0$ is the minimum value of $\sum_{k=1}^{n+1} s_k$ (over all $n$ and $s_1,\ldots,s_{n+1}$), achieved by $(s_1,\ldots,s_{n+1}) = (s_1^0,\ldots,s_{n+1}^0)$. Observe that there cannot be another $(s_1,\ldots,s_{n'+1})$ with the same sum, $\sum_{k=1}^{n'+1} s_k = T_0$, satisfying $g(s_1,\ldots,s_{n'+1}) > 4045$; otherwise, the function $f$ for $(s_1,\ldots,s_{n'+1})$ would satisfy $f(t_0+T_0) > 4045$ and there would be some $T<T_0$ such that $f(t_0+T) = 4045$ by the intermediate value theorem.

We claim that $s_{n+1}^0 \geq 1$ and $s_k^0 = 1$ for $1\leq k\leq n$. If $s_{n+1}^0<1$ then
\begin{align*}
& g(s_1^0,\ldots,s_{n-1}^0,s_n^0+s_{n+1}^0)-g(s_1^0,\ldots,s_{n-1}^0,s_n^0,s_{n+1}^0) \\
&\quad = s_{n+1}^0(2ns_n^0-s_{n+1}^0) > 0,
\end{align*}
contradicting our observation from the previous paragraph. Thus $s_{n+1}^0 \geq 1$. If $s_k^0>1$ for some $1\leq k\leq n$ then replacing $(s_k^0,s_{n+1}^0)$ by $(1,s_{n+1}^0+s_k^0-1)$ increases $g$:
\begin{align*}
&g(s_1^0,\ldots,1,\ldots,s_{n+1}^0+s_k^0-1)-g(s_1^0,\ldots,s_k^0,\ldots,s_{n+1}^0) \\
&\quad= (s_k^0-1)((n+1-k)(s_k^0+1)+2(n+1)(s_{n+1}^0-1)) > 0,
\end{align*}
again contradicting the observation. This establishes the claim.

Given that $s_k^0 = 1$ for $1 \leq k \leq n$, we have
$T = s_{n+1}^0 + n$ and
\[
g(s_1^0,\dots,s_{n+1}^0) = \frac{n(n+1)}{2} + (n+1)(T-n)^2.
\]
Setting this equal to 4045 and solving for $T$ yields
\[
T = n+\sqrt{\frac{4045}{n+1} - \frac{n}{2}}.
\]
For $n=9$ this yields $T = 29$; it thus suffices to show that for all $n$, 
\[
n+\sqrt{\frac{4045}{n+1} - \frac{n}{2}} \geq 29.
\]
This is evident for $n \geq 30$. For $n \leq 29$, rewrite the claim as
\[
\sqrt{\frac{4045}{n+1} - \frac{n}{2}} \geq 29-n;
\]
we then obtain an equivalent inequality by squaring both sides:
\[
\frac{4045}{n+1} - \frac{n}{2} \geq n^2-58n+841.
\]
Clearing denominators, gathering all terms to one side, and factoring puts this in the form
\[
(9-n)(n^2 - \frac{95}{2} n + 356) \geq 0.
\]
The quadratic factor $Q(n)$ has a minimum at $\frac{95}{4} = 23.75$
and satisfies $Q(8) = 40, Q(10) = -19$; it is thus positive for $n \leq 8$ and negative for $10 \leq n \leq 29$.

descriptive_long.solution:
The minimum value of $\totalspan$ is 29.

Write $\postendtime = \starttime+\totalspan$ and define $\spandiff = \intermtime-\previoustime$ for $1\leq \stageindex\leq \segcount+1$. On $[\previoustime,\intermtime]$, we have $\funcvalue'(\elapsedtime) = \stageindex(\elapsedtime-\previoustime)$ and so $\funcvalue(\intermtime)-\funcvalue(\previoustime) = \frac{\stageindex}{2} \spandiff^2$. Thus if we define
\[
\quadraticsum(\firstspan,\ldots,\lastspan) = \sum_{\stageindex=1}^{\segcount+1} \stageindex\spandiff^2,
\]
then we want to minimize $\sum_{\stageindex=1}^{\segcount+1} \spandiff = \totalspan$ (for all possible values of $\segcount$) subject to the constraints that $\quadraticsum(\firstspan,\ldots,\lastspan) = 4045$ and $\spandiff \geq 1$ for $\stageindex \leq \segcount$.

We first note that a minimum value for $\totalspan$ is indeed achieved. To see this, note that the constraints $\quadraticsum(\firstspan,\ldots,\lastspan) = 4045$ and $\spandiff \geq 1$ place an upper bound on $\segcount$. For fixed $\segcount$, the constraint $\quadraticsum(\firstspan,\ldots,\lastspan) = 4045$ places an upper bound on each $\spandiff$, whence the set of $(\firstspan,\ldots,\lastspan)$ on which we want to minimize $\sum \spandiff$ is a compact subset of $\mathbb{\auxiliaryr}^{\segcount+1}$.

Now say that $\minimumspan$ is the minimum value of $\sum_{\stageindex=1}^{\segcount+1} \spandiff$ (over all $\segcount$ and $\firstspan,\ldots,\lastspan$), achieved by $(\firstspan,\ldots,\lastspan) = (\firstspan^0,\ldots,\lastspan^0)$. Observe that there cannot be another $(\firstspan,\ldots,\lastspan)$ with the same sum, $\sum_{\stageindex=1}^{\segcount'+1} \spandiff = \minimumspan$, satisfying $\quadraticsum(\firstspan,\ldots,\lastspan) > 4045$; otherwise, the function $\funcvalue$ for $(\firstspan,\ldots,\lastspan)$ would satisfy $\funcvalue(\starttime+\minimumspan) > 4045$ and there would be some $\totalspan<\minimumspan$ such that $\funcvalue(\starttime+\totalspan) = 4045$ by the intermediate value theorem.

We claim that $\lastspan^0 \geq 1$ and $\spandiff^0 = 1$ for $1\leq \stageindex\leq \segcount$. If $\lastspan^0<1$ then
\begin{align*}
& \quadraticsum(\firstspan^0,\ldots,\penultspan^0,\endspan^0+\lastspan^0)-\quadraticsum(\firstspan^0,\ldots,\penultspan^0,\endspan^0,\lastspan^0) \\
&\quad = \lastspan^0(2\segcount\endspan^0-\lastspan^0) > 0,
\end{align*}
contradicting our observation from the previous paragraph. Thus $\lastspan^0 \geq 1$. If $\spandiff^0>1$ for some $1\leq \stageindex\leq \segcount$ then replacing $(\spandiff^0,\lastspan^0)$ by $(1,\lastspan^0+\spandiff^0-1)$ increases $\quadraticsum$:
\begin{align*}
&\quadraticsum(\firstspan^0,\ldots,1,\ldots,\lastspan^0+\spandiff^0-1)-\quadraticsum(\firstspan^0,\ldots,\spandiff^0,\ldots,\lastspan^0) \\
&\quad= (\spandiff^0-1)((\segcount+1-\stageindex)(\spandiff^0+1)+2(\segcount+1)(\lastspan^0-1)) > 0,
\end{align*}
again contradicting the observation. This establishes the claim.

Given that $\spandiff^0 = 1$ for $1 \leq \stageindex \leq \segcount$, we have
$\totalspan = \lastspan^0 + \segcount$ and
\[
\quadraticsum(\firstspan^0,\dots,\lastspan^0) = \frac{\segcount(\segcount+1)}{2} + (\segcount+1)(\totalspan-\segcount)^2.
\]
Setting this equal to 4045 and solving for $\totalspan$ yields
\[
\totalspan = \segcount+\sqrt{\frac{4045}{\segcount+1} - \frac{\segcount}{2}}.
\]
For $\segcount=9$ this yields $\totalspan = 29$; it thus suffices to show that for all $\segcount$,
\[
\segcount+\sqrt{\frac{4045}{\segcount+1} - \frac{\segcount}{2}} \geq 29.
\]
This is evident for $\segcount \geq 30$. For $\segcount \leq 29$, rewrite the claim as
\[
\sqrt{\frac{4045}{\segcount+1} - \frac{\segcount}{2}} \geq 29-\segcount;
\]
we then obtain an equivalent inequality by squaring both sides:
\[
\frac{4045}{\segcount+1} - \frac{\segcount}{2} \geq \segcount^2-58\segcount+841.
\]
Clearing denominators, gathering all terms to one side, and factoring puts this in the form
\[
(9-\segcount)(\segcount^2 - \frac{95}{2} \segcount + 356) \geq 0.
\]
The quadratic factor $\auxquadratic(\segcount)$ has a minimum at $\frac{95}{4} = 23.75$ and satisfies $\auxquadratic(8) = 40, \auxquadratic(10) = -19$; it is thus positive for $\segcount \leq 8$ and negative for $10 \leq \segcount \leq 29$.

descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
The minimum value of $glenstream$ is 29.

Write $canyonridge = meadowrise+glenstream$ and define $fernshadow = brooktrail-riverbend$ for $1\leq pebblestep\leq lanterns+1$. On $[riverbend,brooktrail]$, we have $maplewind'(sundialer) = pebblestep(sundialer-riverbend)$ and so $maplewind(brooktrail)-maplewind(riverbend) = \frac{\pebblestep}{2} fernshadow^{2}$. Thus if we define
\[
silkgrove(cedarbluff,\ldots,pinehollow) = \sum_{pebblestep=1}^{lanterns+1} pebblestep\, fernshadow^{2},
\]
then we want to minimize $\sum_{pebblestep=1}^{lanterns+1} fernshadow = glenstream$ (for all possible values of $lanterns$) subject to the constraints that $silkgrove(cedarbluff,\ldots,pinehollow) = 4045$ and $fernshadow \geq 1$ for $pebblestep \leq lanterns$.

We first note that a minimum value for $glenstream$ is indeed achieved. To see this, note that the constraints $silkgrove(cedarbluff,\ldots,pinehollow) = 4045$ and $fernshadow \geq 1$ place an upper bound on $lanterns$. For fixed $lanterns$, the constraint $silkgrove(cedarbluff,\ldots,pinehollow) = 4045$ places an upper bound on each $fernshadow$, whence the set of $(cedarbluff,\ldots,pinehollow)$ on which we want to minimize $\sum fernshadow$ is a compact subset of $\mathbb{R}^{\lanterns+1}$.

Now say that $valleydawn$ is the minimum value of $\sum_{pebblestep=1}^{lanterns+1} fernshadow$ (over all $lanterns$ and $cedarbluff,\ldots,pinehollow$), achieved by $(cedarbluff,\ldots,pinehollow) = (cedarbluff^{0},\ldots,pinehollow^{0})$. Observe that there cannot be another $(cedarbluff,\ldots,s_{n'+1})$ with the same sum, $\sum_{pebblestep=1}^{n'+1} fernshadow = valleydawn$, satisfying $silkgrove(cedarbluff,\ldots,s_{n'+1}) > 4045$; otherwise, the function $maplewind$ for $(cedarbluff,\ldots,s_{n'+1})$ would satisfy $maplewind(meadowrise+valleydawn) > 4045$ and there would be some $glenstream<valleydawn$ such that $maplewind(meadowrise+glenstream) = 4045$ by the intermediate value theorem.

We claim that $pinehollow^{0} \geq 1$ and $fernshadow^{0} = 1$ for $1\leq pebblestep\leq lanterns$. If $pinehollow^{0}<1$ then
\begin{align*}
& silkgrove(cedarbluff^{0},\ldots,grovefield^{0},thicketbay^{0}+pinehollow^{0})-silkgrove(cedarbluff^{0},\ldots,grovefield^{0},thicketbay^{0},pinehollow^{0}) \
&\quad = pinehollow^{0}(2\lanterns\, thicketbay^{0}-pinehollow^{0}) > 0,
\end{align*}
contradicting our observation from the previous paragraph. Thus $pinehollow^{0} \geq 1$. If $fernshadow^{0}>1$ for some $1\leq pebblestep\leq lanterns$ then replacing $(fernshadow^{0},pinehollow^{0})$ by $(1,pinehollow^{0}+fernshadow^{0}-1)$ increases $silkgrove$:
\begin{align*}
&silkgrove(cedarbluff^{0},\ldots,1,\ldots,pinehollow^{0}+fernshadow^{0}-1)-silkgrove(cedarbluff^{0},\ldots,fernshadow^{0},\ldots,pinehollow^{0}) \
&\quad= (fernshadow^{0}-1)((\lanterns+1-pebblestep)(fernshadow^{0}+1)+2(\lanterns+1)(pinehollow^{0}-1)) > 0,
\end{align*}
again contradicting the observation. This establishes the claim.

Given that $fernshadow^{0} = 1$ for $1 \leq pebblestep \leq lanterns$, we have
$glenstream = pinehollow^{0} + lanterns$ and
\[
silkgrove(cedarbluff^{0},\dots,pinehollow^{0}) = \frac{\lanterns(\lanterns+1)}{2} + (\lanterns+1)(glenstream-\lanterns)^2.
\]
Setting this equal to 4045 and solving for $glenstream$ yields
\[
glenstream = \lanterns+\sqrt{\frac{4045}{\lanterns+1} - \frac{\lanterns}{2}}.
\]
For $\lanterns=9$ this yields $glenstream = 29$; it thus suffices to show that for all $\lanterns$, 
\[
\lanterns+\sqrt{\frac{4045}{\lanterns+1} - \frac{\lanterns}{2}} \geq 29.
\]
This is evident for $\lanterns \geq 30$. For $\lanterns \leq 29$, rewrite the claim as
\[
\sqrt{\frac{4045}{\lanterns+1} - \frac{\lanterns}{2}} \geq 29-\lanterns;
\]
we then obtain an equivalent inequality by squaring both sides:
\[
\frac{4045}{\lanterns+1} - \frac{\lanterns}{2} \geq \lanterns^2-58\lanterns+841.
\]
Clearing denominators, gathering all terms to one side, and factoring puts this in the form
\[
(9-\lanterns)(\lanterns^2 - \frac{95}{2} \lanterns + 356) \geq 0.
\]
The quadratic factor $woodlark(\lanterns)$ has a minimum at $\frac{95}{4} = 23.75$
and satisfies $woodlark(8) = 40, woodlark(10) = -19$; it is thus positive for $\lanterns \leq 8$ and negative for $10 \leq \lanterns \leq 29$.

descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
The minimum value of $\infiniteperiod$ is 29.

Write $\preinitialmoment = \finalmoment+\infiniteperiod$ and define $\overlapvalue = \irrelevanttime-\nexttimeindex$ for $1\leq \aggregate\leq \infinitevalue+1$. On $[\nexttimeindex,\irrelevanttime]$, we have $\constantvalue'(\spacedimension) = \aggregate(\spacedimension-\nexttimeindex)$ and so $\constantvalue(\irrelevanttime)-\constantvalue(\nexttimeindex) = \frac{\aggregate}{2} \overlapvalue^2$. Thus if we define
\[
\difference(\overlapfirst,\ldots,\overlaplast) = \sum_{\aggregate=1}^{\infinitevalue+1} \aggregate\overlapvalue^2,
\]
then we want to minimize $\sum_{\aggregate=1}^{\infinitevalue+1} \overlapvalue = \infiniteperiod$ (for all possible values of $\infinitevalue$) subject to the constraints that $\difference(\overlapfirst,\ldots,\overlaplast) = 4045$ and $\overlapvalue \geq 1$ for $\aggregate \leq \infinitevalue$.

We first note that a minimum value for $\infiniteperiod$ is indeed achieved. To see this, note that the constraints $\difference(\overlapfirst,\ldots,\overlaplast) = 4045$ and $\overlapvalue \geq 1$ place an upper bound on $\infinitevalue$. For fixed $\infinitevalue$, the constraint $\difference(\overlapfirst,\ldots,\overlaplast) = 4045$ places an upper bound on each $\overlapvalue$, whence the set of $(\overlapfirst,\ldots,\overlaplast)$ on which we want to minimize $\sum \overlapvalue$ is a compact subset of $\mathbb{\imaginarynumbers}^{\infinitevalue+1}$.

Now say that $\maximalperiod$ is the minimum value of $\sum_{\aggregate=1}^{\infinitevalue+1} \overlapvalue$ (over all $\infinitevalue$ and $\overlapfirst,\ldots,\overlaplast$), achieved by $(\overlapfirst,\ldots,\overlaplast) = (\overlapfirst^0,\ldots,\overlaplast^0)$. Observe that there cannot be another $(\overlapfirst,\ldots,\overlaplast)$ with the same sum, $\sum_{\aggregate=1}^{\infinitevalue+1} \overlapvalue = \maximalperiod$, satisfying $\difference(\overlapfirst,\ldots,\overlaplast) > 4045$; otherwise, the function $\constantvalue$ for $(\overlapfirst,\ldots,\overlaplast)$ would satisfy $\constantvalue(\finalmoment+\maximalperiod) > 4045$ and there would be some $\infiniteperiod<\maximalperiod$ such that $\constantvalue(\finalmoment+\infiniteperiod) = 4045$ by the intermediate value theorem.

We claim that $\overlaplast^0 \geq 1$ and $\overlapvalue^0 = 1$ for $1\leq \aggregate\leq \infinitevalue$. If $\overlaplast^0<1$ then
\begin{align*}
& \difference(\overlapfirst^0,\ldots,\overlapmiddle^0+\overlaplast^0)-\difference(\overlapfirst^0,\ldots,\overlapmiddle^0,\overlaplast^0) \\
&\quad = \overlaplast^0(2\infinitevalue\overlapmiddle^0-\overlaplast^0) > 0,
\end{align*}
contradicting our observation from the previous paragraph. Thus $\overlaplast^0 \geq 1$. If $\overlapvalue^0>1$ for some $1\leq \aggregate\leq \infinitevalue$ then replacing $(\overlapvalue^0,\overlaplast^0)$ by $(1,\overlaplast^0+\overlapvalue^0-1)$ increases $\difference$:
\begin{align*}
&\difference(\overlapfirst^0,\ldots,1,\ldots,\overlaplast^0+\overlapvalue^0-1)-\difference(\overlapfirst^0,\ldots,\overlapvalue^0,\ldots,\overlaplast^0) \\
&\quad= (\overlapvalue^0-1)((\infinitevalue+1-\aggregate)(\overlapvalue^0+1)+2(\infinitevalue+1)(\overlaplast^0-1)) > 0,
\end{align*}
again contradicting the observation. This establishes the claim.

Given that $\overlapvalue^0 = 1$ for $1 \leq \aggregate \leq \infinitevalue$, we have
$\infiniteperiod = \overlaplast^0 + \infinitevalue$ and
\[
\difference(\overlapfirst^0,\dots,\overlaplast^0) = \frac{\infinitevalue(\infinitevalue+1)}{2} + (\infinitevalue+1)(\infiniteperiod-\infinitevalue)^2.
\]
Setting this equal to 4045 and solving for $\infiniteperiod$ yields
\[
\infiniteperiod = \infinitevalue+\sqrt{\frac{4045}{\infinitevalue+1} - \frac{\infinitevalue}{2}}.
\]
For $\infinitevalue=9$ this yields $\infiniteperiod = 29$; it thus suffices to show that for all $\infinitevalue$, 
\[
\infinitevalue+\sqrt{\frac{4045}{\infinitevalue+1} - \frac{\infinitevalue}{2}} \geq 29.
\]
This is evident for $\infinitevalue \geq 30$. For $\infinitevalue \leq 29$, rewrite the claim as
\[
\sqrt{\frac{4045}{\infinitevalue+1} - \frac{\infinitevalue}{2}} \geq 29-\infinitevalue;
\]
we then obtain an equivalent inequality by squaring both sides:
\[
\frac{4045}{\infinitevalue+1} - \frac{\infinitevalue}{2} \geq \infinitevalue^2-58\infinitevalue+841.
\]
Clearing denominators, gathering all terms to one side, and factoring puts this in the form
\[
(9-\infinitevalue)(\infinitevalue^2 - \frac{95}{2} \infinitevalue + 356) \geq 0.
\]
The quadratic factor $\linearpart(\infinitevalue)$ has a minimum at $\frac{95}{4} = 23.75$
and satisfies $\linearpart(8) = 40, \linearpart(10) = -19$; it is thus positive for $\infinitevalue \leq 8$ and negative for $10 \leq \infinitevalue \leq 29$.}

garbled_string.solution:
The minimum value of $keznprat$ is 29.

Write $pxlrgkoi = nlvqjfsa+keznprat$ and define $plcyrnuf = wqghybru-ujvrkcen$ for $1\leq oayfnlze\leq hqeslzpw+1$. On $[ujvrkcen,wqghybru]$, we have $zrtcqhyp'(gbsauvdy) = oayfnlze(gbsauvdy-ujvrkcen)$ and so $zrtcqhyp(wqghybru)-zrtcqhyp(ujvrkcen) = \frac{oayfnlze}{2} plcyrnuf^2$. Thus if we define
\[
vxsodmci(yzskdqme,\ldots,ivbopgwl) = \sum_{oayfnlze=1}^{hqeslzpw+1} oayfnlze\, plcyrnuf^2,
\]
then we want to minimize $\sum_{oayfnlze=1}^{hqeslzpw+1} plcyrnuf = keznprat$ (for all possible values of $hqeslzpw$) subject to the constraints that $vxsodmci(yzskdqme,\ldots,ivbopgwl) = 4045$ and $plcyrnuf \geq 1$ for $oayfnlze \leq hqeslzpw$.

We first note that a minimum value for $keznprat$ is indeed achieved. To see this, note that the constraints $vxsodmci(yzskdqme,\ldots,ivbopgwl) = 4045$ and $plcyrnuf \geq 1$ place an upper bound on $hqeslzpw$. For fixed $hqeslzpw$, the constraint $vxsodmci(yzskdqme,\ldots,ivbopgwl) = 4045$ places an upper bound on each $plcyrnuf$, whence the set of $(yzskdqme,\ldots,ivbopgwl)$ on which we want to minimize $\sum plcyrnuf$ is a compact subset of $\mathbb{R}^{hqeslzpw+1}$.

Now say that $syjpwqld$ is the minimum value of $\sum_{oayfnlze=1}^{hqeslzpw+1} plcyrnuf$ (over all $hqeslzpw$ and $yzskdqme,\ldots,ivbopgwl$), achieved by $(yzskdqme,\ldots,ivbopgwl) = (yzskdqme^{0},\ldots,ivbopgwl^{0})$. Observe that there cannot be another $(yzskdqme,\ldots,ivbopgwl)$ with the same sum, $\sum_{oayfnlze=1}^{hqeslzpw'+1} plcyrnuf = syjpwqld$, satisfying $vxsodmci(yzskdqme,\ldots,ivbopgwl) > 4045$; otherwise, the function $zrtcqhyp$ for $(yzskdqme,\ldots,ivbopgwl)$ would satisfy $zrtcqhyp(nlvqjfsa+syjpwqld) > 4045$ and there would be some $keznprat<syjpwqld$ such that $zrtcqhyp(nlvqjfsa+keznprat) = 4045$ by the intermediate value theorem.

We claim that $ivbopgwl^{0} \geq 1$ and $plcyrnuf^{0} = 1$ for $1\leq oayfnlze \leq hqeslzpw$. If $ivbopgwl^{0}<1$ then
\begin{align*}
& vxsodmci(yzskdqme^{0},\ldots,qprbltaz^{0},dwexfchu^{0}+ivbopgwl^{0})-vxsodmci(yzskdqme^{0},\ldots,qprbltaz^{0},dwexfchu^{0},ivbopgwl^{0}) \
&\quad = ivbopgwl^{0}(2 hqeslzpw \, dwexfchu^{0}-ivbopgwl^{0}) > 0,
\end{align*}
contradicting our observation from the previous paragraph. Thus $ivbopgwl^{0} \geq 1$. If $plcyrnuf^{0}>1$ for some $1\leq oayfnlze \leq hqeslzpw$ then replacing $(plcyrnuf^{0},ivbopgwl^{0})$ by $(1,ivbopgwl^{0}+plcyrnuf^{0}-1)$ increases $vxsodmci$:
\begin{align*}
&vxsodmci(yzskdqme^{0},\ldots,1,\ldots,ivbopgwl^{0}+plcyrnuf^{0}-1)-vxsodmci(yzskdqme^{0},\ldots,plcyrnuf^{0},\ldots,ivbopgwl^{0}) \
&\quad= (plcyrnuf^{0}-1)\big((hqeslzpw+1-oayfnlze)(plcyrnuf^{0}+1)+2(hqeslzpw+1)(ivbopgwl^{0}-1)\big) > 0,
\end{align*}
again contradicting the observation. This establishes the claim.

Given that $plcyrnuf^{0} = 1$ for $1 \leq oayfnlze \leq hqeslzpw$, we have
$keznprat = ivbopgwl^{0} + hqeslzpw$ and
\[
vxsodmci(yzskdqme^{0},\dots,ivbopgwl^{0}) = \frac{hqeslzpw(hqeslzpw+1)}{2} + (hqeslzpw+1)(keznprat-hqeslzpw)^2.
\]
Setting this equal to 4045 and solving for $keznprat$ yields
\[
keznprat = hqeslzpw+\sqrt{\frac{4045}{hqeslzpw+1} - \frac{hqeslzpw}{2}}.
\]
For $hqeslzpw=9$ this yields $keznprat = 29$; it thus suffices to show that for all $hqeslzpw$, 
\[
hqeslzpw+\sqrt{\frac{4045}{hqeslzpw+1} - \frac{hqeslzpw}{2}} \geq 29.
\]
This is evident for $hqeslzpw \geq 30$. For $hqeslzpw \leq 29$, rewrite the claim as
\[
\sqrt{\frac{4045}{hqeslzpw+1} - \frac{hqeslzpw}{2}} \geq 29-hqeslzpw;
\]
we then obtain an equivalent inequality by squaring both sides:
\[
\frac{4045}{hqeslzpw+1} - \frac{hqeslzpw}{2} \geq hqeslzpw^2-58 hqeslzpw+841.
\]
Clearing denominators, gathering all terms to one side, and factoring puts this in the form
\[
(9-hqeslzpw)(hqeslzpw^2 - \frac{95}{2} hqeslzpw + 356) \geq 0.
\]
The quadratic factor $xjzamvne(hqeslzpw)$ has a minimum at $\frac{95}{4} = 23.75$ and satisfies $xjzamvne(8) = 40, xjzamvne(10) = -19$; it is thus positive for $hqeslzpw \leq 8$ and negative for $10 \leq hqeslzpw \leq 29$.

kernel_variant.question:
Let n be a non-negative integer and let  

  t_0<t_1<\cdots <t_n                    (\star )

be real numbers that satisfy the spacing condition  

  t_k-t_{k-1} \geq  3  (1 \leq  k \leq  n).          (1)

For t \geq  t_0 define a real-valued function f by  

(a)  f is continuous on [t_0,\infty ) and three times differentiable on (t_0,\infty ) except (possibly) at the mesh points t_1,\ldots ,t_n;  

(b)  f(t_0)=0 and, for every k=0,1,\ldots ,n,
   lim_{t\to t_k^{+}} f'(t)=0 and lim_{t\to t_k^{+}} f''(t)=0;  

(c)  for 1 \leq  k \leq  n the third derivative is constant on (t_{k-1},t_k) and equals  

     f'''(t)=6k,  

  whereas for t>t_n it equals  

     f'''(t)=6(n+1).                 (2)

(The values of f''' at the mesh-points t_0,t_1,\ldots ,t_n are irrelevant.)

Among all admissible choices of n and of the sequence (t_0,\ldots ,t_n) fulfilling (\star )-(2)

(i) determine the least real number T for which one can have  
  f(t_0+T)=998 655;

(ii) for that minimal value T list precisely the integers n for which such a construction is possible.
kernel_variant.solution:
Throughout write  

  C:=998 655, t_{\,n+1}:=t_0+T, s_k:=t_k-t_{k-1}\;(1\leq k\leq n+1).  (3)

Hence  

  T=\sum_{k=1}^{n+1}s_k,  s_k\geq 3\;(1\leq k\leq n),  s_{n+1}>0.  (4)



1.  Cubic constraint.  
On (t_{k-1},t_k) we have f'''(t)=6k and f'(t_{k-1}^{+})=f''(t_{k-1}^{+})=0; three successive integrations give  

  f(t_k)-f(t_{k-1})=k\,s_k^3.  (5)

Summation over k=1,\ldots ,n+1 yields the single constraint  

  g(s_1,\ldots ,s_{n+1}):=\sum_{k=1}^{n+1}k\,s_k^3=C.  (6)



2.  Existence of a minimiser.  
Because of (4)-(6) every s_k is bounded above and below; for fixed n the feasible set is compact.  Inequality (13) below bounds n, so a global minimiser exists.



3.  Kuhn-Tucker equations.  
Introduce \lambda \in \mathbb{R} for (6) and \mu _k\geq 0 for the side conditions s_k-3\geq 0 (1\leq k\leq n).  Stationarity gives  

 1+3\lambda k\,s_k^2+\mu _k=0 (1\leq k\leq n),  (7a)  
 1+3\lambda (n+1)\,s_{n+1}^2=0.    (7b)

From (7b) \lambda <0.  Put  

 \alpha :=-1/(3\lambda )>0, \beta :=\alpha /(n+1).    (8)



4.  Structure of an optimal vector.  
If s_k>3 then \mu _k=0 and (7a) gives k s_k^2=\alpha ,
while for s_k=3 one has \mu _k>0.  Thus the set  

  F:={k\mid s_k>3}  

is an initial segment {1,\ldots ,m} (possibly empty) and  

  s_k=\sqrt{\alpha /k} (1\leq k\leq m),  s_k=3 (m<k\leq n),  s_{n+1}=\sqrt{\beta} . (9)



5.  Why m=0 (explicit computation).  
Assume m\geq 1 and pick \ell :=m, the largest index with s_\ell >3.  
Set  

  \delta :=s_\ell -3>0,  a:=\ell (s_\ell ^3-27)/(n+1)>0.  

Keep all spacings except the two concerned and define the competitor  

  s'_\ell :=3, s'_{n+1}:=(s_{n+1}^3+a)^{1/3}, s'_k:=s_k (k\neq \ell ,n+1). (10)

Because a exactly compensates the loss \ell (s_\ell ^3-27) in (6), the vector s' is feasible.  
Its travelling time satisfies  

  \Delta T:=T'-T=(s'_\ell -s_\ell )+(s'_{n+1}-s_{n+1})=-\delta +[(s_{n+1}^3+a)^{1/3}-s_{n+1}]. (11)

The map x\mapsto x^{1/3} is concave, hence  

  (s_{n+1}^3+a)^{1/3}-s_{n+1} \leq  a/(3s_{n+1}^2).  (12)

Using s_{n+1}^2=\beta =\alpha /(n+1) and s_\ell ^2=\alpha /\ell  we obtain  

 a/(3s_{n+1}^2)=\frac{\ell (s_\ell ^3-27)}{3(n+1)}\frac{n+1}{\alpha }
      = \frac{\ell }{3\alpha }(\alpha ^{3/2}\ell ^{-3/2}-27)
      = \frac{1}{3}\bigl(\sqrt{\alpha} /\sqrt{\ell} -27\ell /\alpha \bigr). (13)

Since \sqrt{\alpha} /\sqrt{\ell} =s_\ell >3, the right-hand side is strictly smaller than \delta =s_\ell -3, and therefore \Delta T<0.  The competitor s' has a shorter travelling time, contradicting optimality.  Thus  

  m=0,\quad s_k=3\;(1\leq k\leq n),\quad s_{n+1}>0.  (14)



6.  Solving the reduced problem.  
With (14) the cubic constraint (6) becomes  

  (n+1)s_{n+1}^3+\frac{27}{2}n(n+1)=C,  (15)

whence  

  s_{n+1}^3=Y_n:=\frac{C-\frac{27}{2}n(n+1)}{\,n+1\,}.  (16)

Positivity of Y_n forces  

  n(n+1)<\frac{2C}{27}=73 974.44\ldots ,  0\leq n\leq 271. (17)

For fixed n the travelling time is  

  T(n)=3n+Y_n^{1/3}.       (18)



7.  Monotonicity of T(n) for n\geq 5.  
Set  

  A_n:=Y_n-Y_{n+1}= \frac{C}{(n+1)(n+2)}+\frac{27}{2}>0. (19)

Because A_n>0, Y_n decreases with n.  By the mean-value theorem applied to t\mapsto t^{1/3} we have  

  Y_n^{1/3}-Y_{n+1}^{1/3}= \frac{A_n}{Y_n^{2/3}+Y_n^{1/3}Y_{n+1}^{1/3}+Y_{n+1}^{2/3}}
        \leq \frac{A_n}{3Y_{n+1}^{2/3}}. (20)

Define  

  \rho (n):=\frac{A_n}{3Y_{n+1}^{2/3}}\qquad(n\geq 5).  (21)

Treating n as a real variable and differentiating ln \rho  one finds  

  \rho '(x)=\frac{d}{dx}\Bigl[\ln A_x-\frac{2}{3}\ln Y_{x+1}\Bigr]<0\quad(x\geq 5), (22)

because -A'/A dominates +(2/3)Y'/Y on that range.  Hence \rho (n) attains its maximum at n=5.  Direct computation gives  

  \rho (5)=\frac{23 791}{3\cdot 142 584^{\,2/3}}\approx 2.879<3, so \rho (n)<3 (n\geq 5). (23)

Therefore  

  Y_n^{1/3}-Y_{n+1}^{1/3}<3  (n\geq 5)     (24)

and consequently  

  \Delta _n:=T(n+1)-T(n)=3+Y_{n+1}^{1/3}-Y_n^{1/3}>0 (n\geq 5). (25)

Thus T(n) is strictly increasing for n\geq 5.



8.  Small values of n.  
Using (16) one obtains  

  T(0)=C^{1/3}\approx 99.96  
  T(1)\approx 82.23 T(2)\approx 75.34 T(3)\approx 71.97  
  T(4)\approx 70.45 T(5)=70.00 T(6)\approx 70.24.  (26)

Hence T(n) decreases strictly up to n=5 and increases afterwards.  The unique global minimum is  

  T_{\min}=70, attained at n=5.  (27)



9.  Explicit extremal data.  
For n=5, equation (16) gives s_6^3=166 375, whence s_6=55.
With s_1=\cdots =s_5=3 we have  

  t_0<t_1<\cdots <t_6,\; t_k-t_{k-1}=3\;(1\leq k\leq 5),\;t_6-t_5=55, T=70. (28)

These values satisfy (\star )-(2), and (5) confirms f(t_0+70)=998 655.



Answer.  
(i) The least possible value of T is 70.  
(ii) This minimum is attained only for n = 5.

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
• Higher-order derivatives:  the original problem deals with a piecewise constant acceleration (second derivative).  We raise the order to a piecewise constant jerk (third derivative), so every interval now involves cubic instead of quadratic motion, inflating algebraic complexity.

• Stronger smoothness conditions:  the function must restart with BOTH zero velocity and zero acceleration after every break, introducing a second set of continuity constraints and forcing an additional optimisation layer.

• Larger minimal spacing and a carefully chosen huge terminal value lead to non-trivial arithmetic (cubic roots of six-figure integers) instead of neat square roots.

• Optimisation requires convex-analysis/majorisation reasoning or Lagrange multipliers in a non-quadratic setting, followed by a delicate one-dimensional minima search whose monotonicity is subtler than in the quadratic case.

• Finally, the problem asks not only for the minimal time but also for a full classification of all indices n that realise it, compelling the solver to prove uniqueness and to exclude borderline competitors—steps absent from the original task.

All these ingredients interact, yielding a problem that is substantially more intricate and technical than the original.

original_kernel_variant.question:
Let n be a non-negative integer and let  

  t_0<t_1<\cdots <t_n                    (\star )

be real numbers that satisfy the spacing condition  

  t_k-t_{k-1} \geq  3  (1 \leq  k \leq  n).          (1)

For t \geq  t_0 define a real-valued function f by  

(a)  f is continuous on [t_0,\infty ) and three times differentiable on (t_0,\infty ) except (possibly) at the mesh points t_1,\ldots ,t_n;  

(b)  f(t_0)=0 and, for every k=0,1,\ldots ,n,
   lim_{t\to t_k^{+}} f'(t)=0 and lim_{t\to t_k^{+}} f''(t)=0;  

(c)  for 1 \leq  k \leq  n the third derivative is constant on (t_{k-1},t_k) and equals  

     f'''(t)=6k,  

  whereas for t>t_n it equals  

     f'''(t)=6(n+1).                 (2)

(The values of f''' at the mesh-points t_0,t_1,\ldots ,t_n are irrelevant.)

Among all admissible choices of n and of the sequence (t_0,\ldots ,t_n) fulfilling (\star )-(2)

(i) determine the least real number T for which one can have  
  f(t_0+T)=998 655;

(ii) for that minimal value T list precisely the integers n for which such a construction is possible.

Throughout write  

  C:=998 655, t_{\,n+1}:=t_0+T, s_k:=t_k-t_{k-1}\;(1\leq k\leq n+1).  (3)

Assume m\geq 1 and pick \ell :=m, the largest index with s_\ell >3.  
Set  

  \delta :=s_\ell -3>0,  a:=\ell (s_\ell ^3-27)/(n+1)>0.

      = \frac{\ell }{3\alpha }(\alpha ^{3/2}\ell ^{-3/2}-27)
      = \frac{1}{3}\bigl(\sqrt{\alpha} /\sqrt{\ell} -27\ell /\alpha \bigr). (13)

Since \sqrt{\alpha} /\sqrt{\ell} =s_\ell >3, the right-hand side is strictly smaller than \delta =s_\ell -3, and therefore \Delta T<0.  The competitor s' has a shorter travelling time, contradicting optimality.  Thus  

  m=0,\quad s_k=3\;(1\leq k\leq n),\quad s_{n+1}>0.  (14)

With (14) the cubic constraint (6) becomes  

  (n+1)s_{n+1}^3+\frac{27}{2}n(n+1)=C,  (15)

whence

  T(n)=3n+Y_n^{1/3}.       (18)

Set  

  A_n:=Y_n-Y_{n+1}= \frac{C}{(n+1)(n+2)}+\frac{27}{2}>0. (19)

  Y_n^{1/3}-Y_{n+1}^{1/3}= \frac{A_n}{Y_n^{2/3}+Y_n^{1/3}Y_{n+1}^{1/3}+Y_{n+1}^{2/3}}

Therefore  

  Y_n^{1/3}-Y_{n+1}^{1/3}<3  (n\geq 5)     (24)

and consequently  

  \Delta _n:=T(n+1)-T(n)=3+Y_{n+1}^{1/3}-Y_n^{1/3}>0 (n\geq 5). (25)

Thus T(n) is strictly increasing for n\geq 5.



8.  Small values of n.  
Using (16) one obtains  

  T(0)=C^{1/3}\approx 99.96  
  T(1)\approx 82.23 T(2)\approx 75.34 T(3)\approx 71.97  
  T(4)\approx 70.45 T(5)=70.00 T(6)\approx 70.24.  (26)

Hence T(n) decreases strictly up to n=5 and increases afterwards.  The unique global minimum is  

  T_{\min}=70, attained at n=5.  (27)



9.  Explicit extremal data.  
For n=5, equation (16) gives s_6^3=166 375, whence s_6=55.
With s_1=\cdots =s_5=3 we have  

  t_0<t_1<\cdots <t_6,\; t_k-t_{k-1}=3\;(1\leq k\leq 5),\;t_6-t_5=55, T=70. (28)

These values satisfy (\star )-(2), and (5) confirms f(t_0+70)=998 655.



Answer.  
(i) The least possible value of T is 70.  
(ii) This minimum is attained only for n = 5.
